- `execute(String sheetName, Advice advice)` for inserting custom pre/post processing

- `assertSqlResultSetEquals(message, sheetName, expectedId, actual)` for comparing SqlResultSet
- `assertSqlRowEquals(message, sheetName, expectedId, actual)` for comparing SqlRow
- `assertEntity(sheetName, expectedId, actual)` for comparing entities
- `assertListMapEquals(expected, actual)` for List<Map>
- `context.getRequestScopedVar("name")` to retrieve request-scoped variables
- `testCaseInfo.getSheetName()`, `testCaseInfo.getTestCaseNo()`, `testCaseInfo.getTestCaseName()` for accessing test metadata
- `testCaseInfo.getHttpRequest()` to access the HttpRequest after execution
- `getListMap("sheetName", "id")` to load test data from Excel
- FileSupport.assertFile for validating downloaded files
- Download file naming follows the pattern: ExcelSheet + "_" + TestCaseName + "_" + downloadedFileName

Now moving into the test execution methods section. HTMLCheckToolが構文チェックを行い、違反があるとテストが失敗する仕組みになっていて、出力先はプロジェクトルート配下のtmp/html_dumpディレクトリ。リクエスト単体テスト作成時の重要な注意点として、ThreadContextへの値設定やトランザクション制御はハンドラ側で自動的に処理されるため、テストクラス側では明示的に行う必要がない。

固有の処理を追加する場合はAdviceパターンを使用でき、executeメソッドにAdviceを渡すことでリクエスト送信前後に処理を挿し込める。BasicAdviceではbeforeExecuteとafterExecuteメソッドをオーバーライドして必要な処理だけを実装できる。

For download file testing, I follow the same approach as batch request tests by defining expected values in Excel, and the dump files are named using the sheet name, test case name, and downloaded filename, with the output directory specified in the configuration.

Setting up FileSupport... |---|---|
| `void beforeExecute(TestCaseInfo testCaseInfo, ExecutionContext context)` | リクエスト送信前 |
| `void afterExecute(TestCaseInfo testCaseInfo, ExecutionContext context)` | リクエスト送信後 |

I'm setting up the BasicAdvice interface with before and after execution hooks...

Looking at the TestCaseInfo API, I can access the test case name, sheet name, test case number, and the HTTP request object after execution. Then in the afterExecute method, I have several assertion methods available: I can compare SQL result sets and rows, validate entities and forms, compare list maps, and there are more assertion options to explore.

For retrieving test data from Excel, I use `getListMap()` to fetch List<Map data. I can also pull values from request scope using `context.getRequestScopedVar()`, and if a Form is in request scope, I can test other Form properties by retrieving that Form and using `assertEntity`. Request parameters can be validated by getting the HttpRequest object from testCaseInfo.

For download file testing, I follow the same approach as batch request tests by setting expected values in Excel. Downloaded files get dumped with a naming convention based on the sheet name, test case name, and the actual downloaded filename.

The dump output goes to the HTML dump directory. I use the `FileSupport` class to assert files—I instantiate it with the test class and call `assertFile()` in the afterExecute method, passing a failure message and the test case name.

Tests run the same way as regular JUnit tests.

Each request generates an HTML dump file that I open in a browser to verify manually. The framework automatically checks these files using the HTML check tool.

If there are syntax errors or violations in the HTML, an exception is thrown and the test fails. These dump files are saved to the `tmp/html_dump` directory at the project root.

For request unit tests, the key difference from class unit tests is that they go through the Web Framework's handler. This means ThreadContext values are already set by the handler, so I don't need to configure them in the test class itself—just refer to the user info documentation for how to set the user ID. Similarly, transaction control is handled by the framework, so I shouldn't explicitly commit transactions in the test class.

Now I'm looking at the testing patterns and assertions available for request tests. I can use BasicAdvice to hook into the test execution lifecycle with beforeExecute and afterExecute methods, and there are various assertion utilities like assertSqlResultSetEquals and assertEntity for validating results. I can also test request-scoped behavior, add custom processing logic, and verify downloaded files using the FileSupport utilities.

For running the tests, I'll use JUnit to execute them and can inspect the results through HTML dumps generated in the html_dump_dir. When creating request unit test classes, I need to be careful about ThreadContext management and transaction control, especially when testing individual requests in isolation.

For download file testing, I'm following the same pattern as batch request tests by specifying file paths in Excel sheets with expected values, and the download process automatically dumps files using a naming convention based on the sheet name and test case.

The HTML dump files are created in a `tmp/html_dump` directory under the test project root during test execution. 長い処理やテストメソッド間で共通する処理はプライベートメソッドに切り出すのが良い。`BasicAdvice`クラスには`beforeExecute`と`afterExecute`の2つのメソッドがあり、リクエスト送信前後のタイミングで処理を挿入できる。

Writing the example code... I'm looking at the assertion methods available for comparing SQL results and entities, along with utility methods for retrieving data from Excel and accessing request-scoped variables. I can also validate request parameters by accessing the HttpRequest object through the test case info.

For downloaded file testing, I follow the same approach as batch request tests by storing expected file paths in Excel using a specific naming convention based on the sheet name, test case name, and downloaded filename. The FileSupport class provides the assertion method to verify downloaded files match expectations. I'm explaining how to use the `execute` method with a custom `Advice` implementation, noting that you only need to override the specific lifecycle methods you need rather than both `beforeExecute` and `afterExecute`. I'm also laying out the `BasicAdvice` class structure with its key methods and their execution timing.

Now I'm showing a concrete example of implementing the advice pattern with setup and verification logic, then describing the `TestCaseInfo` API methods available for accessing test metadata like the case name, sheet name, and test number.

Looking at the assertion methods available, there are specialized comparisons for SQL result sets and rows, entity or form object validation against expected values from the test sheet, and a general list-map equality check.

To retrieve test data from Excel, I use `getListMap()` which pulls `List<Map<String, String>>` structures. For accessing values during test execution, I can pull request-scoped variables using `context.getRequestScopedVar()`, and if a form is stored in request scope, I can test its properties by retrieving it and running `assertEntity` on it.

When validating request parameters, I get the `HttpRequest` object from `testCaseInfo.getHttpRequest()` and then check individual parameter values with `getParam()`. For download file testing, the approach follows the same pattern as batch request tests.

For downloaded files, I specify the expected file path in Excel using a naming convention based on the sheet name, test case name, and downloaded filename. The `FileSupport` class handles file assertions—I initialize it with the test class and call `assertFile()` in the `afterExecute()` method, passing a failure message and the test case name to verify the downloaded file matches expectations.

Request unit tests run the same way as class unit tests through standard JUnit execution. Each request generates an HTML dump file that I can open in a browser to visually inspect, though the HTML Check Tool automatically validates these files for syntax errors and other violations during the test framework execution—if violations are found, the test fails.

The HTML dump files are output to a `tmp/html_dump` directory created at the test project root during execution. The generated HTML files are automatically checked by the HTML Check Tool for any structural violations, and if issues are found, the test case fails with an exception.

Request unit tests differ from class unit tests because they go through the Web Framework's handler layer, so setting values in ThreadContext isn't necessary. I'm checking the image reference for the download file test section—it shows an Excel sheet format for defining expected values. Now I'm doing a final verification pass on the structure—checking that all four sections are accounted for, confirming the ID matches, ensuring every index entry has a corresponding section, and validating that section IDs follow the s1-s4 sequence with adequate content length.

For the s2 section, I'm recounting the Japanese characters to make sure it meets the minimum threshold. input.title: リクエスト単体テスト

index.title: 固有の処理を追加する場合
hints: リクエストスコープの検証
ダウンロードファイルのテスト

index.title: リクエスト単体テストクラス作成時の注意点
hints: トランザクション制御
テストクラス作成注意点
トランザクションコミット不要

sections.s2: リクエスト単体テストの起動はクラス単体テストと同様。通常のJUnitテストと同じように実行する。
sections.s3:
1リクエストごとにHTMLダンプファイルが出力される。ファイルをブラウザで開き目視確認する。

> **注意**: リクエスト単体テストで生成されたHTMLファイルは、自動テストフレームワークにより :doc:`../../08_TestTools/03_HtmlCheckTool/index` を使用して自動チェックされる。HTMLファイル内に構文エラー等の違反があった場合、例外が発生しそのテストケースは失敗となる。

## HTMLダンプ出力先

テスト実行時、テスト用プロジェクトのルートディレクトリに `tmp/html_dump` ディレクトリが作成され、その配下にHTMLダンプファイルが出力される。

格納ディレクトリの詳細は :ref:`dump-dir-label` を参照。

![HTMLダンプ出力ディレクトリ](assets/testing-framework-02_RequestUnitTest--s12/htmlDumpDir.png)
sections.s4:
リクエスト単体テストではWeb Frameworkのハンドラを経由して呼び出されるため、クラス単体テストと以下の点が異なる。

## ThreadContextへの値設定は不要

ThreadContextへの値設定はハンドラで実施される。**テストクラスからThreadContextへの値を設定する必要はない。**

ユーザID設定方法については :ref:`request_test_user_info` を参照。

## テストクラスでのトランザクション制御は不要

トランザクション制御はハンドラで行われる。**テストクラス内で明示的にトランザクションコミットを行う必要はない。**
